title: Superconducting properties of Nb$_{0.85}$Sc$_{0.15}$ film deposited by magnetron co-sputtering
authors: Paul Berezhnoy, Anna Elistratova, Zakhar Enbaev, Mikhail Dryazgov, Alexander Mumlyakov, Oleg Solovyev, Mikael Geodakyan, Igor Trofimov, Vasily Stolyarov, Alexander Korneev, Michael Tarkhov
abstract: The technology has been developed for synthesizing Nb$_{1-x}$Sc$_x$ films using magnetron co-sputtering from Nb and Sc targets. The material synthesis was accompanied by structural characterization using X-ray diffraction and X-ray reflectometry methods, which enabled the determination of thickness, phase composition and crystal structure. We also analyzed the superconducting properties. The critical temperature $T_c$ was measured for samples with different concentarions of Sc and Nb. The maximum value of $T_c$ equal to 6.35 K was observed for sample with a scandium content of approximately 15 %, which was determined by Auger spectroscopy. Transport and magnetoresistive measurements were performed in microbridges with length of 50 $μ$m, width of 2 $μ$m, and thickness of 30 nm. The critical current density was as high as 2.5 $\text{ MA/cm}^{2}$. Magnetic measurements were performed with the field oriented perpendicular to the sample. The upper critical field $H_{c2}(0) = 3.2$ T, electron diffusion coefficient $D = 1.1$ $\text{cm}^{2}/s$, and coherence length $ξ_{GL} = 10.1$ nm. The synthesized Nb$_{1-x}$Sc$_x$ intermetallic compound shows promise for various functional cryogenic electronics devices. \keywords{NbSc; superconductor; microbridge}
summary: This paper reports on the successful synthesis and comprehensive characterization of superconducting $	ext{Nb}_{1-x}	ext{Sc}_x$ thin films using magnetron co-sputtering. Key findings include a maximum critical temperature of 6.35 K and high critical current densities, demonstrating the material's promise for advanced functional cryogenic electronic devices.
main_problem: The study focuses on synthesizing and characterizing the superconducting properties of $	ext{Nb}_{1-x}	ext{Sc}_x$ intermetallic thin films.
main_result: The synthesized $	ext{Nb}_{1-x}	ext{Sc}_x$ film exhibits superconductivity with a maximum critical temperature ($T_c$) of 6.35 K and a high critical current density ($J_c$) up to $2.5 	ext{ MA/cm}^2$.
method: The research employed magnetron co-sputtering for film deposition, followed by structural characterization using XRD/XRR and electrical measurements (transport and magnetic) to probe superconducting parameters.
model_or_system: The physical system is the $	ext{Nb}_{1-x}	ext{Sc}_x$ intermetallic compound thin film, which is analyzed as a superconducting material suitable for cryogenic electronics.
key_observables: Critical temperature ($T_c$), critical current density ($J_c$), upper critical field ($H_{c2}$), electron diffusion coefficient ($D$), and coherence length ($\xi_{GL}$).
important_parameters: Maximum $T_c$ of 6.35 K at $\approx 15\% 	ext{ Sc}$; $J_c$ up to $2.5 	ext{ MA/cm}^2$; $H_{c2}(0) = 3.2 	ext{ T}$.
main_assumptions: The systematic shifts in diffraction angles suggest a substitutional solid solution or metastable phase, while depth profiling suggests the lattice parameter shifts are due to strain rather than composition gradients.
figures_summary: Figures show the co-sputtering process, microbridge fabrication, XRR curves indicating thickness non-uniformity, and GIXRD patterns demonstrating peak shifts dependent on the incidence angle.
paper_structure: The paper details the synthesis process, structural characterization (XRD/XRR/AES), and comprehensive superconducting property measurements ($T_c$, $J_c$, $H_{c2}$, etc.) on samples with varying Sc concentrations.
why_interesting: While primarily experimental, the detailed characterization of superconducting parameters ($T_c$, $H_{c2}$, $\xi_{GL}$) and the comparison to other superconducting materials (like $	ext{NbN}$) provide fundamental data relevant to understanding superconducting mechanisms in engineered thin films.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
